questions.question: At which OSI layer does TCP operate, and what is its primary responsibility?
options: Layer 4 (Transport) — reliable end-to-end delivery, sequencing, and retransmission between hosts
Layer 2 (Data Link) — MAC addressing and switching within a LAN
Layer 7 (Application) — HTTP headers and JSON payloads
Layer 1 (Physical) — voltage on the wire
questions.explanation: TCP is Transport layer (L4). SRE troubleshooting maps symptoms to layers: L4 issues include connection resets, retransmits, and port reachability.

questions.question: You can ping a host but HTTPS fails with connection timeout. Which OSI layers are most relevant to investigate first?
options: Layer 3 (routing/IP) and Layer 4 (TCP/443) — ICMP may work while TCP to port 443 is blocked or filtered
Layer 7 only — ping proves the application is fine
Layer 1 only — replace the cable
Layer 8 — political problems exclusively
questions.explanation: Ping uses ICMP (L3). HTTPS needs TCP L4 to 443 plus TLS/app above. Reddit wc4ryg notes networking depth beyond kernel trivia — layer thinking is core.

questions.question: DNS resolution failures most directly map to which OSI layer in troubleshooting frameworks?
options: Layer 7 (Application) — name-to-address mapping service, though it sits above UDP/TCP (L4)
Layer 2 — DNS is an Ethernet protocol
Layer 1 — DNS runs on fiber only
Layer 5 — DNS is exclusively session layer with no transport
questions.explanation: DNS is an application-layer service (L7) using UDP/TCP transport. SRE interviews expect you to connect DNS symptoms to resolver config, TTL, and upstream connectivity.

questions.question: Path MTU black holes often cause TLS handshakes to hang mid-connection. Which layers are involved?
options: Layer 3 IP fragmentation and Layer 2 MTU — large packets dropped without ICMP 'fragmentation needed'
Layer 7 only — TLS bug in OpenSSL
Layer 5 session timeout exclusively
Layer 1 clock skew
questions.explanation: PMTUD spans L3/L2. Symptoms look like app hangs (L7) but root cause is MTU/fragmentation — classic SRE layer separation skill.

questions.question: Which OSI layer does a load balancer performing TCP connection termination primarily operate at?
options: Layer 4 (Transport) — accepts TCP connections and forwards to backends
Layer 1 — amplifies electrical signals only
Layer 7 always — L4 load balancers do not exist
Layer 3 — load balancers only route IP packets without ports
questions.explanation: L4 LB works on IP+port (TCP/UDP). L7 LB inspects HTTP headers. Knowing the distinction is standard systems/network interview material.

questions.question: When troubleshooting latency, mapping 'high TCP retransmit rate' to OSI helps because retransmits are primarily a:
options: Layer 4 transport symptom — may indicate loss, congestion, or middlebox interference on the path
Layer 7 application bug only
Layer 2 ARP cache aesthetic issue
Layer 1 power supply failure
questions.explanation: Retransmits are observed at L4 (TCP). SREs correlate with L3 routing, L2 errors, or upstream congestion — not jump straight to app code.

questions.source: reddit:z5c3m7; LPI; Google SRE SE prep
questions.question: What happens when a parent process calls fork() and the child calls exec()?
options: fork() clones the process; exec() replaces the child's memory image with a new program — classic spawn pattern
fork() creates a thread; exec() kills the parent
exec() must run before fork() always
fork() duplicates only the network stack, not memory
questions.explanation: Reddit z5c3m7 recommends process lifecycle fundamentals over kernel struct trivia. fork+exec is the Unix spawn model every SRE-SE candidate should explain.

questions.question: A process stuck in uninterruptible sleep (D state) usually indicates:
options: Waiting on I/O (often disk) — cannot be killed until kernel completes the I/O operation
Waiting for user input at the terminal
Normal idle state — D means 'Done'
CPU spinning at 100% in user space
questions.explanation: D-state processes block in kernel on I/O. Reddit/HN SRE rubrics cite checking D-state when CPU looks idle but workloads hang — storage or NFS issues.

questions.question: What is the main difference between virtual memory and physical RAM from an application’s perspective?
options: Each process sees a private virtual address space mapped by the MMU to physical frames — enabling isolation and overcommit
Virtual memory is slower disk only; RAM is never used
They are identical terms
Virtual memory exists only in containers, not on Linux hosts
questions.explanation: Reddit 1fkwupo stresses fundamentals over tools. Virtual memory + page faults explain OOM, swap thrashing, and mmap behavior in interviews.

questions.question: A disk reports 100% full but `df` shows space available. What Linux filesystem concept explains inode exhaustion?
options: All inodes are allocated — many small files can fill inode table while bytes remain
df is always wrong on ext4
Inodes only exist on Windows NTFS
The kernel auto-deletes files when inodes run low
questions.explanation: Inode vs block exhaustion is classic Linux internals. SRE interviews expect you to check `df -i` and know implications for apps creating millions of small files.

questions.question: Why might strace show a process repeatedly calling futex() with high latency?
options: Lock contention — futex backs userspace mutexes; threads block waiting for a contended lock
DNS failure at Layer 7
futex always means memory leak
The process is idle and futex is normal sleep with zero cost
questions.explanation: wc4ryg: explain implications, not memorizing struct fields. futex + lock contention connects syscalls to performance debugging.

questions.question: A container hits OOMKilled despite the host having free RAM. Most likely Linux mechanism?
options: cgroup memory limit enforced by the kernel — container exceeded its cgroup max, not host RAM
Docker randomly kills containers
OOM only happens when disk is full
Virtual memory is disabled in containers
questions.explanation: cgroup v1/v2 memory limits cause OOM kill inside the group. SRE-SE candidates tie Linux internals to Kubernetes/production symptoms.

questions.question: During TCP three-way handshake, which flag combination does the server send in the second step (SYN-ACK)?
options: SYN and ACK both set — acknowledges client SYN and sends server SYN
FIN and RST
ACK only with no SYN
PSH and URG
questions.explanation: TCP state machine basics appear in Google SRE SE networking depth per Reddit wc4ryg. SYN-ACK is step 2 of handshake.

questions.question: Intermittent 'Could not resolve host' errors affect one AZ only. Best first networking check?
options: Compare resolver config (/etc/resolv.conf, systemd-resolved) and reachability to DNS servers from affected AZ
Rebuild the application binary
Clear browser cache on laptops
Assume DNS is global and ignore AZ
questions.explanation: DNS is infrastructure. SRE troubleshooting starts with resolver paths, VPC DNS endpoints, and L3 reachability to nameservers.

questions.question: curl works to IP:443 but fails to hostname:443. What does this narrow down?
options: DNS or hostname resolution problem — TCP/IP path to the IP is likely fine
TLS certificate expired only when using hostnames
Layer 1 cable fault
HTTP Layer 7 bug exclusively
questions.explanation: Classic divide: IP works, name fails → DNS or SNI-related naming. Reddit troubleshooting threads emphasize systematic elimination.

questions.question: Why does DNS commonly use UDP for queries (with TCP fallback for large responses)?
options: UDP is lightweight for small request/response — lower overhead than TCP for simple lookups
UDP guarantees delivery unlike TCP
DNS cannot use TCP by design
UDP operates at Layer 2 only
questions.explanation: DNS over UDP:53 for typical queries; TCP when truncation occurs. Know transport choice for SRE networking questions.

questions.question: High network latency between two hosts in the same region but different subnets. OSI-aware checklist item?
options: Trace L3 path (traceroute), check ACLs/security groups (L3/L4), and NIC/driver errors (L1/L2)
Only restart the application
Latency in-region is always zero — ignore network
Check CSS files first
questions.explanation: o6ozcj confirms Linux/networking focus for SRE-SE. Same-region latency implicates routing, firewalls, or NIC issues across L1–L4.

questions.question: A busy proxy runs out of ephemeral ports causing connection failures. Linux tuning concept?
options: Expand ip_local_port_range and reduce TIME_WAIT duration / enable connection reuse — exhaustion at L4
Increase inode count
Disable TCP entirely
Add more RAM to fix port exhaustion
questions.explanation: Ephemeral port exhaustion is L4 (TCP quadruple). SREs know sysctl tuning and connection pooling — bridges Linux internals and networking.
